era_context: The early-to-mid 8th century CE is a period of dynastic consolidation and religious transformation. The Tang Dynasty dominates East Asia; the Umayyad Caliphate is entering a period of decline; the Byzantine Empire is consolidating iconoclastic policies; and the Frankish Kingdom is preparing for the Carolingian transition. This is a transitional decade before major upheavals that would reshape Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (trade routes documented but local political events poorly attested)

events.title: Death of Leo III; accession of Constantine V as Byzantine Emperor
events.description: Leo III (r. 717–741 CE), founder of the Isaurian Dynasty and initiator of the Iconoclastic Controversy, dies in 741 CE. He is succeeded by his son Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE), who would become one of the most controversial figures in Byzantine history. Constantine V is known for his military prowess and his aggressive enforcement of iconoclasm. The transition from Leo III to Constantine V marks a continuation and intensification of iconoclastic policies, which would dominate Byzantine religious and political life for over a century.

sources.name: Theophanes the Confessor, Chronographia, compiled early 9th century
Nikephoros I, Patriarch of Constantinople, Breviarium, 9th century

events.certainty_note: Leo III's death in 741 CE and Constantine V's accession are well-attested in Byzantine historiography. The date is confirmed in multiple sources.

events.title: Frankish military campaigns under Pepin III
events.description: Pepin III (the Short), mayor of the palace and de facto ruler of the Frankish Kingdom, continues military campaigns to consolidate Frankish power. In 741 CE, Frankish forces are active in various theaters, including campaigns against Aquitaine, Saxony, and other neighboring territories. These campaigns are part of the broader Carolingian expansion that would culminate in Pepin's deposition of the last Merovingian king (751 CE).
key_figures: Pepin III (the Short, Mayor of the Palace)
Childeric III (Last Merovingian King, nominal authority)

sources.name: Annales Regni Francorum (Annals of the Frankish Kingdom), compiled 9th century
Liber Historiae Francorum (Book of Frankish History), 8th century

events.certainty_note: Frankish military activity in this period is attested in Carolingian annals, though specific campaigns in 741 CE are sometimes conflated with adjacent years.

events.title: Umayyad Caliphate under Hisham II; provincial fragmentation
events.region: Umayyad Caliphate (Damascus and wider Islamic world)
events.description: Hisham II (r. 724–743 CE) continues to rule the Umayyad Caliphate during a period of increasing internal instability and provincial fragmentation. The caliphate is fragmenting, particularly in Iberia and North Africa, where Berber and Arab factions are competing for power. The central authority of the caliphate is weakening, and regional governors are becoming increasingly independent. This fragmentation is a key factor that would enable the Abbasid Revolution's success just a few years later.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 10th century
Ibn al-Athir, Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh, compiled 13th century

events.certainty_note: Hisham II's reign and the Umayyad Caliphate's internal instability and provincial fragmentation are well-attested in Islamic historiography.

events.title: Tang Dynasty administrative and cultural flourishing
events.description: The Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong continues its period of cultural and administrative flourishing. The 740s CE represent the height of Tang power and influence, with extensive trade networks (including the Silk Road), military dominance in East and Central Asia, and cultural prestige. Poetry, painting, and other arts flourish at the Tang court. This stability and prosperity would be disrupted by the An Lushan Rebellion (755–763 CE).

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 10th century
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: The Tang Dynasty's cultural and administrative activities in the 740s CE are well-attested in Chinese historiography, though specific events in 741 CE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.

events.title: Abbasid movement organizing in Khorasan and Central Asia
events.description: The Abbasid movement, a revolutionary Shi'a-influenced faction opposed to Umayyad rule, continues its organizational work in Khorasan and Central Asia. By 741 CE, the Abbasid da'wa (missionary organization) is well-established among disaffected Persian, Khorasani, and Shi'a populations. This organizational work, though not yet openly revolutionary, is laying the groundwork for the Abbasid Revolution that would formally begin in 747 CE and succeed by 750 CE.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings, compiled 10th century

events.certainty_note: The Abbasid movement's organizational phase is attested in later Islamic historiography, but contemporary documentation is minimal. The exact timeline of the da'wa's development is reconstructed from later sources and is subject to scholarly debate.

disconfirming_evidence: Leo III's death in 741 CE is well-attested, but the exact date is sometimes given as 740 or 741 CE in different sources. Constantine V's accession is confirmed, but the transition between the two emperors is sometimes dated to 740 or 742 CE in different sources. Hisham II's reign and the Umayyad Caliphate's internal instability are well-attested, but the extent to which these factors directly caused the caliphate's collapse (as opposed to being one factor among many) is debated. Frankish campaigns in 741 CE are attested only in general terms; specific military engagements are not documented. The Abbasid movement's organizational work in 741 CE is inferred from later sources and is not directly attested in contemporary documentation.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 741 CE is moderate but unevenly distributed. Islamic sources (compiled in the 10th–13th centuries) provide narrative frameworks for Umayyad and Abbasid history, though contemporary documentation is sparse. Byzantine sources exist but are often polemical regarding iconoclasm. Frankish sources are sparse and compiled centuries later. Chinese sources are relatively rich but focus on court and administrative matters. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are almost entirely absent from contemporary documentation. The surviving sources are predominantly elite-focused and reflect the biases of later compilers. Archaeological evidence for this year is limited.

graph_edges.note: Constantine V's accession in 741 CE begins his reign, which lasts until 775 CE
Constantine V's accession continues the Iconoclastic Controversy initiated by Leo III in 726 CE
Umayyad provincial fragmentation in 741 CE contributes to the caliphate's collapse in 750 CE
Abbasid organizational work in 741 CE leads to formal revolution in 747 CE
